cards.prompt: A SemaphoreSlim(2) caps concurrent access at 2 regardless of how many tasks start; the max observed concurrency is 2
cards.code: using System; using System.Threading; using System.Threading.Tasks; var sem = new SemaphoreSlim(2); int cur = 0, max = 0; object m = new object(); var tasks = new Task[8]; for (int i=0;i<8;i++) tasks[i]=Task.Run(async ()=>{ await sem.WaitAsync(); lock(m){ cur++; if(cur>max) max=cur; } await Task.Delay(20); lock(m){ cur--; } sem.Release(); }); Task.WaitAll(tasks); Console.WriteLine($"maxConcurrent={max}");

cards.prompt: Mutex and Semaphore derive from WaitHandle (native OS handle); SemaphoreSlim is the lightweight alternative and does not
cards.code: using System; using System.Threading; bool mutexWH = typeof(Mutex).IsSubclassOf(typeof(WaitHandle)); bool semWH = typeof(Semaphore).IsSubclassOf(typeof(WaitHandle)); bool slimWH = typeof(SemaphoreSlim).IsSubclassOf(typeof(WaitHandle)); Console.WriteLine($"{mutexWH} {semWH} {slimWH}");

cards.prompt: AutoResetEvent is a turnstile: one Set releases exactly one waiting thread, so one Set lets one through and three Sets let all three through
cards.code: using System; using System.Threading; var are = new AutoResetEvent(false); int passed = 0; var done = new CountdownEvent(3); for(int i=0;i<3;i++){ var t = new Thread(()=>{ are.WaitOne(); Interlocked.Increment(ref passed); done.Signal(); }); t.Start(); } Thread.Sleep(50); int afterOneSet = 0; are.Set(); Thread.Sleep(50); afterOneSet = passed; are.Set(); are.Set(); done.Wait(); Console.WriteLine($"afterOneSet={afterOneSet} total={passed}");
